coffees.name: Honduras Comayagua Santa Lucia Reserve Organic
coffees.processNote: Washed, then dried in the sun through Comayagua's long clear season. The Santa Lucia Reserve parcels are certified organic and double as a bird habitat — Raul's favorite is the toucan.
coffees.aboutFarm:
Finca Santa Lucia sits outside the little village of Tres Pinos, two and a half hours from Comayagua, the nearest city of any size. Raul Rodriguez runs it now, third generation, living there with his wife and three dogs. He inherited the farm from his father, Don Ermenegildo Rodriguez, one of the great coffee innovators of his time, who introduced the region to exotic varietals like Yellow Bourbon, Typica, and Caturra back when almost nobody local was thinking that way about specialty coffee. The farm sits inside Montaña de La Choca, a national mountain reserve, wrapped in cloud forest full of exotic wildlife. The seasons here are sharply defined, and that matters more than people think: the rains build beautiful cherry, and the long clear dry stretch afterward gives the producers the weather they need to sun-dry the washed coffee properly, while the altitude and the rich clay soil keep the integrity of the bean intact from start to finish.

Raul is always pushing — for better coffee and for a better life for the people who grow it. In the highest corners of his land he's trialing new varietals, Ovata and Pacamara, and his newest parcel, Santa Lucia Reserve, is certified organic and kept as a habitat for birds, including his favorite, the toucan. The farm employs around 75 pickers each harvest and 15 permanent workers who live on the farm with their families, and Raul pays the highest salaries in the region. This year part of the profit from his direct sales goes to rebuilding the local school, which badly needs a kitchen and electricity.

journey.label: Finca Santa Lucia · Comayagua, Honduras
journey.story: This bean grew at about 1,700 meters on the slopes of Montaña de Comayagua, a cloud-forest national park in central Honduras. Comayagua is one of the six coffee regions IHCAFE recognizes, and the forest above these farms is where the water comes from.

coffees.name: Kayanza Masha Anaerobic Natural
coffees.processNote: Whole cherries sealed in tanks with no oxygen, then dried in the sun with the fruit still on. That is where the fizz and the stone fruit come from. It is supposed to taste like that.
coffees.aboutFarm: The Masha washing station buys cherry from over 2,500 farmers in its community, which sounds like an enormous number until you understand why. More than 80% of coffee farmers in Burundi are smallholders, meaning most of them are tending a couple hundred trees, producing less than a single pallet of specialty grade coffee each season. That's not enough for any buyer to build a relationship around on its own. So stations like Masha do the work of gathering all those small harvests, sorting them, and blending them into lots big enough to actually export and build a flavor profile around. Every bag of this is really thousands of tiny harvests standing on each other's shoulders.

journey.label: Masha Station · Kayanza, Burundi
journey.story: Masha is a collection station in the hills of Gatara commune, Kayanza, in northern Burundi near the Rwandan border. Thousands of smallholder farmers each bring cherry from a small plot of red bourbon trees. At 1,672 meters, this is some of the highest coffee country in East Africa.

coffees.name: Veracruz Finca La Laja
coffees.station: Finca La Laja · Tlaltetela, Huatusco highlands
coffees.processNote: Shade-grown under the cloud forest canopy on a farm the Sampieri family has worked since 1920.
coffees.aboutFarm: Finca La Laja sits in the La Laja community in Tlaltetela, up in the central mountains of Veracruz, somewhere between 1,130 and 1,250 meters. The whole area is fed by small tributary rivers running down into the Rio La Antigua, and the vegetation shifts as you climb: cloud forest of pine and oak higher up, giving way to tropical deciduous forest lower down. You can taste some of that range in the cup. It opens with a gentle citrus brightness and a little sweetness up front, then settles into a full, creamy body with a buttery finish that lingers without ever turning heavy. Brown sugar, toffee, cashew butter, a little lemon meringue, and underneath all of it a floral note that never quite steps forward enough to name.

journey.label: Finca La Laja · Veracruz, Mexico
journey.story: La Laja sits in the cloud forests of central Veracruz, in the mountains above Huatusco, watered by small rivers that feed the Río La Antigua. The Sampieri family has grown coffee on these slopes since 1920. On a clear day, Pico de Orizaba stands on the horizon.

coldBrew.name: One Batch Cold Brew
coldBrew.aboutFarm:
Origin TBD, but it'll be something worth the wait. Steeped 18 to 24 hours, half a gallon of water to 220 grams of grounds, a ratio strong enough that you're meant to dilute it rather than wonder where the flavor went. What comes out the other end is thick and dark, closer to a concentrate than a coffee, even though nobody around here calls it that.

Cut it with cream and a splash of water if you want it easy. A touch of sweetener if that's your thing, no judgment. Or pour it straight over ice, no water, no cream, and let it hit the way it's supposed to. That's how I drink the first glass of every batch, standing at the counter, deciding if this one's a keeper.

Brewed once, one batch, by the half gallon. When it's gone, it's gone until the next one.
